title: The Importance of Spike Lee
format: A solo tribute ahead of Da 5 Bloods: Sean's top five Spike Lee films, a replayed Rewatchables conversation with Wesley Morris about Do the Right Thing, and interviews with two of Lee's longest-running collaborators, composer Terrence Blanchard and editor Barry Alexander Brown.

segments.heading: Sean's Top Five Spike Lee Films
segments.note: recorded amid the George Floyd protests, ahead of Da 5 Bloods
segments.subject: Spike Lee

films.title: Do the Right Thing
films.note: No. 1. Maybe the best movie of the 1980s, and maybe the most essential text on what it means to live in and be a citizen of New York.

films.title: 25th Hour
films.note: No. 2. Written by David Benioff from his own novel; Ed Norton's Monty Brogan spends his last day before prison. One of the most impactful movies of this century, and proof Spike works on a broader canvas than he's given credit for.

films.title: Crooklyn
films.note: Tied for No. 3 with Clockers. A personal reflection of Lee's family life, co-written with his sisters, and one of the most touching movies Sean can recall.

films.title: Clockers
films.note: Tied for No. 3 with Crooklyn. Still a story of family and community, but also of crime and policing, with Mekhi Phifer, Harvey Keitel, John Turturro, and a towering Delroy Lindo.

films.title: Inside Man
films.note: No. 4. Originally conceived as a Ron Howard movie; Lee turns it into his version of Dog Day Afternoon, working openly in Sidney Lumet's mold, with Denzel Washington and Jodie Foster.

films.title: Bamboozled
films.note: No. 5. An extravagant satire: Damon Wayans's Black TV executive, pushed by his white studio boss (Michael Rapaport), reinvents the minstrel show for the new millennium. Recently reissued by Criterion.

segments.heading: Do the Right Thing, revisited
segments.note: replayed from a 2019 Rewatchables conversation with Wesley Morris

films.title: Do the Right Thing
films.note: The Wikipedia synopsis reduces it to the pizzeria wall of fame, but that dispute over whose photos hang there really is the whole movie: who gets a foothold in a neighborhood, and who's given one just by being who they are. David Denby's contemporary review in New York Magazine called the ending 'a shambles'; Morris and Fennessey call it a perfect, deliberately dialectical film that nobody 'wins.'

films.title: Red Hook Summer
films.note: Mookie reappears years later, delivering pizza for a relocated Sal's. Lee insisted it wasn't a sequel to Do the Right Thing, but the choice clearly says Brooklyn doesn't change.

films.title: Mo' Better Blues
films.note: Blanchard's first collaboration with Lee, coaching Denzel Washington's trumpet fingering scene by scene.

films.title: Jungle Fever
films.note: Blanchard's first score for Lee: he played Spike a solo piano piece he was writing for the murdered children of Soweto, and it became the film's love theme after Spike overheard it.

films.title: Miracle at St. Anna
films.note: Lee sent Blanchard set-still screensavers before footage existed; Blanchard had to rewrite the entire opening battle sequence once Lee asked for something more emotional and melodic instead of action music.

films.title: Inside Man
films.note: Blanchard tried to signal a delicate love theme by scoring it for piano and flute; Lee heard it and made it the main theme instead.

films.title: BlacKkKlansman
films.note: Blanchard calls it the culmination of decades of work with Lee.

films.title: Da 5 Bloods
films.note: Built around Marvin Gaye; the four-minute-49-second opening sequence alone took Blanchard five days to write and, he says, will go down as a classic.

films.title: 25th Hour
films.note: Its drum sound came up again while Blanchard was scoring Da 5 Bloods.

segments.heading: Barry Alexander Brown on cutting Spike

films.title: She's Gotta Have It
films.note: Brown cut one scene; Lee cut the rest himself.

films.title: School Daze
films.note: The first film Lee actually hired Brown to cut in full.

films.title: Do the Right Thing
films.note: Neither of them knew editors were supposed to cut during the shoot, so Brown didn't touch the footage until Lee wrapped, then cut the whole film in six weeks.

films.title: Malcolm X
films.note: The film after which Brown finally started to think of himself as an editor rather than a writer or documentarian who happened to cut.

films.title: Summer of Sam
films.note: One of the run of films Lee and Brown shot on film with nightly projected dailies, before the shift to digital.

films.title: The Original Kings of Comedy
films.note: Their first digital feature, cut with an assistant, Kim Chisholm, who'd never worked on film before.

films.title: BlacKkKlansman
films.note: Cut fast on Avid; the Kwame Ture campus-speech portraits and the Harry Belafonte/Birth of a Nation intercut were both built in days, not weeks, with Lee tweaking after the first pass.

films.title: 25th Hour
films.note: Shot partly on film even as they went digital: a frame-rate mismatch in the final Brian Cox monologue forced Brown back onto an old-fashioned work print to fix it.

films.title: Salaam Bombay!
films.note: Brown edited Mira Nair's film in the same period he was cutting for Lee; it earned a foreign-language Oscar nomination.

films.title: Madonna: Truth or Dare
films.note: Brown cut Madonna's tour documentary not long after Do the Right Thing.

films.title: The War at Home
films.note: Brown's own Oscar-nominated documentary about 1960s Madison, Wisconsin, which he calls the film school he never had.

films.title: Son of the South
films.note: Brown's own film, in pre-production at the time of this interview: Bob Zellner, a white Alabama college graduate radicalized by the 1961 Freedom Riders.

films.title: Spider-Man: Into the Spider-Verse
films.note: Brown's answer to the show's closing question ('the last great thing you've seen'), calling it, animation or not, some of the best editing around.

TV: If God Is Willing and da Creek Don't Rise
Opera: Fire Shut Up in My Bones
